Monod - The Modeler's Notebook and Datastore

Monod is a web-based environment supporting collaborative work by scientists studying complex biological systems. The immediate application is to the Alpha pheromone signal transduction pathway in S. Cerevisiae.

Monod is open-source under the LGPL. You can download the binaries and source code, or try the demo. Also, please subscribe to our mailing lists, monod-announce and monod-discuss

Motivation

Molecular biologists have a great and increasing need for communicating large amounts of highly detailed information about the interactions between proteins, DNA, RNA, and other contents of cells. Such information is traditionally written up in journal articles. These are written in natural language, without imposing a formal data structure, and they are often focussed on some particular detail of the system in question. There is at present no mechanism for communicating, discussing, and collaboratively editing a comprehensive formal model of a biological system.

Of course, some scientists have built their own models and simulations on the basis of their own understanding of the literature. These are labor-intensive for one individual to construct, are typically limited to a set of differential equations, and are often not readily available to or easily editable by anyone but the author.

Thus, what is needed is an intermediate step between journal papers and simulation equations: a rich environment wherein multiple scientists can navigate and edit a somewhat formalized but not overly rigid model of a system, along with annotations, citations, and links to related information.

Current Functions

We have implemented such a system, and are beginning to use it productively. Our implementation is a web application available through a web browser.

The software allows browsing and editing a reaction network. It provides a detail page for every molecular species and for every biological process or reaction.

The species page for the protein "Ste4", for instance, lists all of the reactions in which Ste4 participates; the compartments in the cell where Ste4 is found, and in what numbers; the possible phosphorylations and other configuration states of Ste4; numerous text annotations; and citations to the literature.

One of the reactions listed is the association of Ste4 and Ste5. Clicking on the reaction produces the corresponding detail page, with links to both Ste4 and Ste5, as well as text annotations and references. The reaction may require Ste4 and Ste5 to be in certain states, and it may alter their state.

All of this information is editable online via web forms.

Clicking on a reference produces a detail page with the full citation, abstract, link to the corresponding PubMed record, and a link to a PDF of the full paper. The citation page also provides links to the molecular species and processes within the model where the paper is cited, as well as text annotations.

The citation database is searchable by author, journal, date, and keywords in the title and abstract.

The system provides fine-grained version control and permissions control. The version control system stores every past revision of each record, allowing the user to view the database as it existed at any previous point in time. The permissions system protects each piece of data from viewing and editing by unauthorized users. Thus, each user can add his or her own data and research results, even if the database already contains contradictory information.

Future Directions

Monod will become far more valuable in practice once different instances of the application can communicate with each other. In general, each individual laboratory will want a local instance of the server; but users may wish to share data entered on one server with users in a different lab (subject to permissions constraints, of course). We are exploring peer-to-peer network architectures to facilitate this communication.

A second focus of development concerns the treatment of states, such as phosphorylations, and complexes of species. A small number of species, each with a small number of binding sites and a small number of states, can easily produce a very large number of distinct complexes, each of which may have a distinct behavior. Representing this situation in a database and building a reasonable user interface for interacting with this kind of data is a non-trivial problem.

Once we have an improved data representation and user interface for states and complexes, we will be able to export models from Monod for import into simulators, such as Moleculizer, as well as visualization tools.

The version and permissions control features will allow the database ultimately to contain many related models of the same biological process, each representing a different viewpoint. From these it will be possible to extract a "consensus" model, or a model based on the results of certain trusted individuals, or a model based on only those results that have passed a peer review procedure, and so on.
